title: Logistic Regression with R
text: Course Overview

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
Who this course is for
If you have completed the Linear Regression Short Course, you have the foundation to build a wide range of statistical models for data analysis. However, linear regression is not suitable for all types of data, and it is common to encounter data in which the outcome variable is binary (e.g., success/failure, yes/no). Logistic regression is a powerful tool for modeling binary outcomes, and this course will guide you through its concepts and applications.

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
What you will learn

Understand the difference between linear and logistic regression.
Recognize when logistic regression is appropriate for binary outcomes.
Translate between probability, odds, and log-odds.
Fit logistic models in R with glm() and read the output.
Interpret coefficients as odds ratios and communicate effects clearly.
Assess model performance with likelihood measures and classification metrics.
Make predictions and evaluate classification accuracy.

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
Prerequisites

You have completed the Linear Regression short course (or equivalent).
You can run basic R scripts and read simple model summaries.

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
Course map

Motivation: modeling binary outcomes when to use logistic regression (and when linear regression fails).
Introduction to Logistic Regression (GLMs, odds/logit, logistic function).
Fitting Logistic Regression Models logistic regression models (estimation, interpretation, inference).
?sec-assessing model fit (deviance, model comparison, pseudo R-squared).
Predictions from Logistic Regression Models from logistic regression models (probabilities, thresholds, ROC/AUC).
?sec-extensions and mini case study (optional: multivariable models, interactions, reporting).

title: Logistic Regression with R
text:
How to use this book

The course is designed to be completed sequentially: successive chapters build on prior concepts. However, it is possible to jump to specific sections as needed.
There is a glossary at the end for quick reference of key terms.
Each chapter includes excersises which should be attempted before continuing.

title: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes
text:
1.0.1 From categories to probabilities
In the Linear Regression Short Course (LRSC), we used a linear model to predict an outcome variable \(Y\) from one or more predictor variables, \(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p\). Something like this:
\[
Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + ... + \epsilon
\]
where \(\epsilon\) is an error term that captures the variability in \(Y\) that is not explained by the predictors. In the cases we considered, Y was a continuous variable (e.g., salary, Naplan score, etc.), and the model described how the expected value of the outcome, \(E[Y]\), changed as the predictors varied. For example, in the case of salary, the model described how the expected salary (\(E[Y]\)) of a worker changed as their years of experience (\(X\)) increased.
In many real-world situations, however, we want to predict an outcome that is not continuous. Instead, often what we want to predict or explain is a categorical variable. The simplest case of this is a binary outcome — that is, an outcome with only two possible categories (e.g. success/failure, yes/no, 0/1) 1.

title: 1  Introduction to Logistic Regression
text:
Example 1.1: A binary outcome: pass or fail
Johno is a student studying for an important exam. He wants to understand how his study time affects his chances of passing the exam. To this end, he collects data on previous students’ study times and whether they passed or failed the exam. Here is a sample of the data Johno collected:


# A tibble: 395 × 2
   studytime pass_fail
       <dbl> <chr>    
 1         2 fail     
 2         2 fail     
 3         2 pass     
 4         3 pass     
 5         2 pass     
 6         2 pass     
 7         2 pass     
 8         2 fail     
 9         2 pass     
10         2 pass     
# ℹ 385 more rows


Johno wants to use this data to model the relationship between study time and the probability of passing the exam. He identifies his outcome variable, \(Y\), as the binary variable indicating whether a student passed or failed the exam, and his predictor variable, \(X\), as the amount of time spent studying. Therefore, Johno’s goal is to produce a statistical model that predicts Y (pass/fail) based on X (study time): \[
Y = f(X) + \varepsilon
\] {.}



1.1.1 From categories to probabilities

1.1.1.1 The average of a binary outcome
Recall that the error term \(\epsilon\) in a regression model captures the difference between the observed outcome \(Y\) and the mean, or expected outcome \(E[Y]\) from the model.

Key-point: Expected value and error term
The expected value of a random variable \(Y\), denoted \(E[Y]\), is the long-run average value of \(Y\) if we were to repeat the process that generates \(Y\) many times. That is, if we were to collect many samples of \(Y\) under the same conditions, the average of those samples would converge to \(E[Y]\). The error term \(\varepsilon\) represents the deviation of an observed value of \(Y\) from its expected value \(E[Y]\).
The Error term \(\varepsilon\) is the difference between the observed value of \(Y\) and its expected value \(E[Y]\) i.e. \(\varepsilon = Y - E[Y]\). It captures the variability in \(Y\) that is not explained by the predictors in the model.
In regression models, we typically assume that the error term \(\varepsilon\) has a mean of zero (i.e., \(E[\varepsilon] = 0\)), which implies that the expected value of \(Y\) given the predictors is equal to the predicted value from the model (i.e., \(E[Y] = f(X)\)).


When \(Y\) was continuous, \(E[Y]\) represented the average value of \(Y\) for given values of the predictors (e.g. the average (AKA expected, or predicted) salary of a worker of 9 years experience). But what is the ‘average’ of a binary (categorical) variable?
To model a binary outcome statistically, we have to represent it as a Random Variable rather than just a label. We can do this by coding the two categories as numbers, e.g. \(0\) and \(1\)2. For example, in Johno’s case

Example 1.2: Coding the binary outcome
We can code the outcome variable \(Y\) as follows: \[
Y =
\begin{cases}
\text{1} & \text{if student passes the exam} \\
\text{0} & \text{if student fails the exam}
\end{cases}
\]
in R,


# A tibble: 395 × 3
   studytime pass_fail pass_fail_num
       <dbl> <chr>             <dbl>
 1         2 fail                  0
 2         2 fail                  0
 3         2 pass                  1
 4         3 pass                  1
 5         2 pass                  1
 6         2 pass                  1
 7         2 pass                  1
 8         2 fail                  0
 9         2 pass                  1
10         2 pass                  1
# ℹ 385 more rows





Continue

The choice of using 0 and 1 here is not arbitrary. While we could have coded the categories as -1 and 1, or 0 and 2, the 0/1 coding has a special property that makes it particularly useful for modeling. In particular, when we code a binary outcome this way, the mean of the outcome variable, \(E[Y]\) has a direct interpretation: \[
E[Y] = P(Y=1) = p
\]
That is,

Key-point
The expected value (mean) of a binary variable coded as 0/1 is the probability of the outcome being 1.


In Johno’s case, \(E[Y]\) represents the probability of passing the exam:

Example 1.3: Mean of a binary variable as a probability
If we ignore study time for a moment and just look at the overall pass/fail rates in Johno’s dataset, we can calculate the mean of the binary outcome variable:

mean(student_performance$pass_fail_num)

[1] 0.6708861


This means that, on average, the probability of a student passing the exam in Johno’s dataset is approximately 67.09%.



Continue




1.1.2 Why not use linear regression?
We now have a way to represent our binary outcome variable as a random variable, and we understand that the expected value of this variable has a meaningful interpretation as a probability.
A natural question at this point is: why not just use linear regression to model the relationship between \(X\) and \(E[Y]\)? After all, probabilities are continuous values between 0 and 1. We could fit a linear model like this: \[
p= E[Y] = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X
\]

Example 1.4: When linear regression is not appropriate
Having completed the Linear Regression Short Course, Johno considers using linear regression to model the relationship between study time and the probability of passing the exam.
He first plots the data in a scatter plot with a fitted linear regression line:









(note the use of geom_jitter() to add some random noise to the points so they don’t overlap exactly).
And obtains the following estimate for the effect of study time on the probability of passing:


# A tibble: 2 × 5
  term        estimate std.error statistic  p.value
  <chr>          <dbl>     <dbl>     <dbl>    <dbl>
1 (Intercept)   0.586     0.0621      9.44 3.46e-19
2 studytime     0.0418    0.0282      1.48 1.39e- 1


The estimated equation for the relationship between study time and the probability of passing is: \[
p = 0.586 + 0.0418 \times \text{studytime}
\]
i.e. The model predicts that a student who does not study at all (study time = 0)3 has a probability of passing of about 0.586, and that for each additional hour of study time, the probability of passing increases by about 0.0418 (i.e. 4.18 percentage points).
In particular, over the range of study times in the dataset, the model produces predicted probabilities of passing that are between 0 and 1:


        1         2         3         4 
0.6694035 0.7112321 0.6275749 0.7530607 


This makes sense since the ‘highest score’ of any student in our dataset is 1 (i.e. a pass), and the ‘lowest score’ is 0 (a fail). No student has a predicted score of exactly 0 or 1, but the predicted values are between these two extremes.
However, Johno is a dedicated student and wants to know what happens if he studies for more hours than any student in the dataset. If we use the linear model to predict probabilities for study times outside the range of the data, we get some nonsensical predictions.
For example, a student who studies for 10 hours has a predicted probability of passing of
predict(model, newdata = data.frame(studytime = 10))
which is greater than 1 (impossible for a probability).


With a binary outcome, the relationship between predictors and probability is typically nonlinear: the same”change in a predictor cannot produce the same change in probability at every starting probability (e.g., you can’t increase a 0.95 probability by +0.2).


1.1.3 from probabilties to odds

title: 1  Motivation: modeling binary outcomes
text:
types of random variables are covered in more detail in the Inferential Statistics Short Course.↩︎
The use of 0/1 coding for binary outcomes is similar to the use of dummy variables for categorical predictors in linear regression, where each category is represented by a separate binary variable coded as 0 or 1.↩︎
Note that no student in the dataset has 0 study time, so this is an extrapolation.↩︎

title: 2  Introduction to Logistic Regression
text: 3 Introduction to Logistic Regression

title: 2  Introduction to Logistic Regression
text:
3.1 Generalised linear models
Logistic regression is a special case of a generalised linear model (GLM):

The outcome distribution is binomial (events out of trials; binary is the 1-trial case)
The mean is linked to a linear predictor via the logit link

title: 2  Introduction to Logistic Regression
text:
3.2 The logistic function
The logistic function maps any real number to a value between 0 and 1. In R, this is plogis().

eta <- c(-4, -2, 0, 2, 4)
data.frame(eta = eta, p = plogis(eta))

  eta          p
1  -4 0.01798621
2  -2 0.11920292
3   0 0.50000000
4   2 0.88079708
5   4 0.98201379

title: 2  Introduction to Logistic Regression
text:
3.3 Binomial distribution
For a single observation with probability of success \(p\), a binary outcome is:
\[
Y \sim \text{Bernoulli}(p)
\]
More generally, for \(n\) trials:
\[
Y \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)
\]

dbinom(x = 0:3, size = 3, prob = 0.4)

[1] 0.216 0.432 0.288 0.064

title: 2  Introduction to Logistic Regression
section: 3.4 From probability to odds and logit
text:
3.4 From probability to odds and logit
If \(p\) is the probability of an event, the odds are \[
\\text{odds} = \\frac{p}{1 - p}
\] The logit is the log of the odds: \[
\\text{logit}(p) = \\log\\left(\\frac{p}{1 - p}\\right)
\]

p <- 0.2
odds <- p / (1 - p)
logit <- log(odds)
c(p = p, odds = odds, logit = logit)

        p      odds     logit 
 0.200000  0.250000 -1.386294 



A change of 1 unit in the logit scale multiplies the odds by about 2.72 (because \(e^1 \\approx 2.72\)).

title: 2  Introduction to Logistic Regression
text:
3.5 The logistic curve
The logit scale is linear, but the probability scale is S-shaped. This is why logistic regression keeps predicted probabilities in the 0 to 1 range.

eta <- seq(-6, 6, length.out = 200)
plot(eta, plogis(eta), type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Linear predictor (eta)", ylab = "Probability")
abline(h = c(0, 1), col = "grey80", lty = 3)

title: 3  Fitting Logistic Regression Models
text: 4 Fitting Logistic Regression Models

title: 3  Fitting Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.1 Maximum likelihood estimation
Logistic regression parameters are typically estimated by maximum likelihood. In R, glm(..., family = binomial) fits logistic regression models using likelihood-based methods.

fit_wt <- glm(am ~ wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial)
logLik(fit_wt)

'log Lik.' -9.588042 (df=2)

title: 3  Fitting Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.2 Fitting a model in R

fit_wt <- glm(am ~ wt, data = mtcars, family = binomial)
summary(fit_wt)


Call:
glm(formula = am ~ wt, family = binomial, data = mtcars)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)   12.040      4.510   2.670  0.00759 **
wt            -4.024      1.436  -2.801  0.00509 **
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 43.230  on 31  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 19.176  on 30  degrees of freedom
AIC: 23.176

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 6



Key-point 3.1
Coefficients are on the log-odds scale. To interpret them as odds ratios, exponentiate with exp().

title: 3  Fitting Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.3 Interpreting coefficients

fit_ex <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial)
coef(fit_ex)

(Intercept)          wt          hp 
 18.8662987  -8.0834752   0.0362556 

exp(coef(fit_ex))

 (Intercept)           wt           hp 
1.561455e+08 3.085967e-04 1.036921e+00 



Exercise 3.1
Compute the odds ratio for wt and store it as or_wt.











or_wt <- exp(coef(fit_ex)["wt"])
or_wt
or_wt <- exp(coef(fit_ex)["wt"])
or_wt

title: 3  Fitting Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.4 Inference in logistic regression
For quick inference, you can use Wald tests from summary() and Wald confidence intervals via confint.default().

summary(fit_ex)


Call:
glm(formula = am ~ wt + hp, family = binomial, data = mtcars)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept) 18.86630    7.44356   2.535  0.01126 * 
wt          -8.08348    3.06868  -2.634  0.00843 **
hp           0.03626    0.01773   2.044  0.04091 * 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 43.230  on 31  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 10.059  on 29  degrees of freedom
AIC: 16.059

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 8

confint.default(fit_ex)

                    2.5 %     97.5 %
(Intercept)   4.277193002 33.4554044
wt          -14.097967884 -2.0689825
hp            0.001497294  0.0710139

title: 3  Fitting Logistic Regression Models
text:
4.5 Categorical predictors

fit_cat <- glm(am ~ wt + factor(cyl), data = mtcars, family = binomial)
summary(fit_cat)


Call:
glm(formula = am ~ wt + factor(cyl), family = binomial, data = mtcars)

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)   
(Intercept)    20.853      8.032   2.596  0.00942 **
wt             -7.859      3.055  -2.573  0.01009 * 
factor(cyl)6    3.105      2.425   1.280  0.20042   
factor(cyl)8    5.379      3.201   1.681  0.09281 . 
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 43.230  on 31  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 14.661  on 28  degrees of freedom
AIC: 22.661

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 7


When a predictor is a factor, coefficients compare each level to a reference level. Interpret them as odds ratios relative to that baseline.

title: 4  Assessing Model Fit
text: 5 Assessing Model Fit

title: 4  Assessing Model Fit
text:
5.1 Deviance
Deviance compares a fitted model to a saturated model (perfect fit). Smaller deviance generally indicates better fit, and differences in deviance can be used for likelihood ratio tests.

fit_null <- glm(am ~ 1, data = mtcars, family = binomial)
fit_full <- glm(am ~ wt + hp + factor(cyl), data = mtcars, family = binomial)

anova(fit_null, fit_full, test = "Chisq")

Analysis of Deviance Table

Model 1: am ~ 1
Model 2: am ~ wt + hp + factor(cyl)
  Resid. Df Resid. Dev Df Deviance  Pr(>Chi)    
1        31     43.230                          
2        27      7.255  4   35.974 2.929e-07 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1



Key-point 4.1
Logistic regression does not use R-squared in the same way as linear regression. Model comparison is typically done with likelihood-based tools (deviance, likelihood ratio tests) and information criteria (AIC).

title: 4  Assessing Model Fit
text:
5.2 Goodness-of-fit tests
Some goodness-of-fit tests (e.g., Hosmer–Lemeshow) require additional packages and choices about grouping. In practice, you should combine multiple checks:

Likelihood-based comparisons between nested models
Predictive performance (next chapter)
Residual and influence diagnostics for unusual points

title: 4  Assessing Model Fit
text:
5.3 Pseudo R-squared
Pseudo R-squared measures (e.g., McFadden’s) are based on likelihoods and are best used for rough comparisons rather than as a direct analogue of linear-model R-squared.

fit_null <- glm(am ~ 1, data = mtcars, family = binomial)
fit_full <- glm(am ~ wt + hp + factor(cyl), data = mtcars, family = binomial)

mcfadden_r2 <- 1 - as.numeric(logLik(fit_full) / logLik(fit_null))
mcfadden_r2

[1] 0.8321671

title: 4  Assessing Model Fit
text:
5.4 AIC

AIC(fit_null, fit_full)

         df      AIC
fit_null  1 45.22973
fit_full  5 17.25537

title: 5  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text: 6 Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
crumbs: 5  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 5  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
6.1 Generating predicted probabilities

fit <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial)
new_cars <- data.frame(wt = c(2.2, 3.2, 3.8), hp = c(110, 110, 110))
predict(fit, newdata = new_cars, type = "response")

           1            2            3 
0.9937615708 0.0468551348 0.0003846502 


You can also visualise how predicted probability changes with a predictor (holding other predictors constant).

fit <- glm(am ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = binomial)
grid <- data.frame(
  wt = seq(min(mtcars$wt), max(mtcars$wt), length.out = 50),
  hp = mean(mtcars$hp)
)
grid$prob <- predict(fit, newdata = grid, type = "response")
plot(grid$wt, grid$prob, type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Weight", ylab = "Predicted probability")
crumbs: 5  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 5  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
6.2 Classification thresholds
If you turn probabilities into classes (0/1), you need a threshold. Changing the threshold trades sensitivity against specificity.

threshold <- 0.5
class_hat <- factor(ifelse(prob >= threshold, 1, 0), levels = c(0, 1))
actual <- factor(mtcars$am, levels = c(0, 1))
conf_mat <- table(Predicted = class_hat, Actual = actual)
conf_mat

         Actual
Predicted  0  1
        0 18  1
        1  1 12



true_pos <- conf_mat["1", "1"]
true_neg <- conf_mat["0", "0"]
false_pos <- conf_mat["1", "0"]
false_neg <- conf_mat["0", "1"]

accuracy <- (true_pos + true_neg) / sum(conf_mat)
sensitivity <- true_pos / (true_pos + false_neg)
specificity <- true_neg / (true_neg + false_pos)

c(accuracy = accuracy, sensitivity = sensitivity, specificity = specificity)

   accuracy sensitivity specificity 
  0.9375000   0.9230769   0.9473684
crumbs: 5  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 5  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models
text:
6.3 ROC curves and AUC

roc_thresholds <- seq(1, 0, by = -0.02)
actual <- factor(mtcars$am, levels = c(0, 1))
actual_num <- as.integer(actual) - 1

tpr <- sapply(roc_thresholds, function(t) {
  pred <- ifelse(prob >= t, 1, 0)
  tp <- sum(pred == 1 & actual_num == 1)
  fn <- sum(pred == 0 & actual_num == 1)
  tp / (tp + fn)
})

fpr <- sapply(roc_thresholds, function(t) {
  pred <- ifelse(prob >= t, 1, 0)
  fp <- sum(pred == 1 & actual_num == 0)
  tn <- sum(pred == 0 & actual_num == 0)
  fp / (fp + tn)
})

roc_df <- data.frame(fpr = fpr, tpr = tpr)
roc_df <- roc_df[order(roc_df$fpr), ]

plot(roc_df$fpr, roc_df$tpr, type = "l", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "False positive rate", ylab = "True positive rate")
abline(0, 1, lty = 3, col = "grey60")







auc <- sum(diff(roc_df$fpr) * (head(roc_df$tpr, -1) + tail(roc_df$tpr, -1)) / 2)
auc

[1] 0.9878543



Key-point 5.1
AUC near 0.5 indicates random guessing, while values closer to 1 indicate stronger discrimination.



Exercise 5.1
Using a threshold of 0.6, compute the accuracy and store it as accuracy_06.











threshold <- 0.6
class_hat <- ifelse(prob >= threshold, 1, 0)
accuracy_06 <- mean(class_hat == actual_num)
accuracy_06
threshold <- 0.6
class_hat <- ifelse(prob >= threshold, 1, 0)
accuracy_06 <- mean(class_hat == actual_num)
accuracy_06
crumbs: 5  Predictions from Logistic Regression Models

title: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study
text: 7 Extensions and a Mini Case Study
crumbs: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study

title: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study
text:
7.1 Learning outcomes

Fit multivariable logistic models with categorical predictors.
Use interactions when effects differ by group.
Summarize results as predicted probabilities for realistic scenarios.
crumbs: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study

title: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study
text:
7.2 Multivariable logistic regression

fit_multi <- glm(am ~ wt + hp + factor(cyl), data = mtcars, family = binomial)
summary(fit_multi)


Call:
glm(formula = am ~ wt + hp + factor(cyl), family = binomial, 
    data = mtcars)

Coefficients:
              Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
(Intercept)   18.09383    9.46271   1.912   0.0559 .
wt           -10.67598    5.44182  -1.962   0.0498 *
hp             0.10321    0.09606   1.074   0.2826  
factor(cyl)6   2.76575    3.15679   0.876   0.3810  
factor(cyl)8  -8.38896   13.16709  -0.637   0.5240  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 43.2297  on 31  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  7.2554  on 27  degrees of freedom
AIC: 17.255

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 9



Key-point 6.1
Each coefficient is a partial effect on the log-odds scale, holding the other predictors constant.
crumbs: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study

title: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study
text:
7.3 Interactions for subgroup effects
If the effect of a predictor changes across groups, use an interaction term.

fit_interaction <- glm(am ~ wt * factor(cyl), data = mtcars, family = binomial)
summary(fit_interaction)


Call:
glm(formula = am ~ wt * factor(cyl), family = binomial, data = mtcars)

Coefficients:
                 Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
(Intercept)        13.758      7.787   1.767   0.0773 .
wt                 -5.116      2.984  -1.714   0.0865 .
factor(cyl)6      313.415  71542.593   0.004   0.9965  
factor(cyl)8       14.544     22.810   0.638   0.5237  
wt:factor(cyl)6  -102.300  23338.031  -0.004   0.9965  
wt:factor(cyl)8    -3.340      6.854  -0.487   0.6261  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 43.230  on 31  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 12.593  on 26  degrees of freedom
AIC: 24.593

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 19
crumbs: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study

title: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study
text:
7.4 Communicating results with scenarios

new_cars <- data.frame(
  wt = c(2.4, 3.2),
  hp = c(100, 150),
  cyl = factor(c(4, 6))
)

predict(fit_multi, newdata = new_cars, type = "response")

        1         2 
0.9422625 0.8982382
crumbs: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study

title: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study
section: 7.5 Mini case study template (dataset to be added)
text:
7.5 Mini case study template (dataset to be added)
Use this checklist to structure the final case study once the dataset is chosen.

Define the outcome variable and clarify what counts as an event.
Summarize the baseline event rate and missing data.
Choose a small set of predictors with clear interpretations.
Fit a logistic model and report odds ratios and predicted probabilities.
Evaluate the model with an appropriate threshold and a confusion matrix.
Communicate results in plain language with scenario predictions.


Note 6.1
The final case study will be updated once the outcome variable is confirmed for the selected research dataset.
crumbs: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study

title: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study
text:
7.6 Reporting template
Use this outline to write up results in plain language.

Outcome definition, sample size, and event rate.
Model specification (predictors and reference levels).
Key effects as odds ratios with a short interpretation.
Predicted probabilities for 2 to 3 realistic scenarios.
Performance summary (threshold, confusion matrix metrics, AUC).
Limitations and next steps.

Template paragraph:
“We modeled [outcome] using logistic regression with predictors [list]. The event rate was [rate]. A one-unit increase in [predictor] was associated with [odds ratio] times the odds of the event, holding other variables constant. For a typical case ([scenario]), the predicted probability was [probability]. Using a threshold of [threshold], the model achieved [accuracy/sensitivity/specificity] and an AUC of [AUC]. Key limitations include [limitations].”
crumbs: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study

title: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study
text:
7.7 Practice prompts

Fit the model with factor(gear) instead of factor(cyl). What changes?
Compare predictions from fit_multi and fit_interaction for the same scenario.
Draft a one-paragraph summary for a non-technical audience.
crumbs: 6  Module 4: Extensions and a Mini Case Study

title: Glossary
text:
Accuracy
The proportion of all predictions that are correct.


Akaike Information Criterion
A model comparison metric that balances fit and complexity; lower values indicate a preferred model among those compared.


AUC
Area under the ROC curve; a summary of classification performance across thresholds.


Binary outcome
A response variable with two possible values, often coded as 0 and 1.


Binomial distribution
A probability distribution for the number of events in repeated binary trials, with event probability p.


Calibration
How closely predicted probabilities match observed event rates.


Class imbalance
When one outcome class is much more frequent than the other.


Classification threshold
The cutoff used to convert predicted probabilities into classes (for example, predict 1 if p >= 0.5).


Coefficient
A model parameter that describes how a predictor changes the log-odds, holding other predictors constant.


Confidence interval
A range of plausible values for a model parameter based on the data.


Confusion matrix
A table of predicted versus actual outcomes used to summarize classification performance.


Deviance
A goodness-of-fit measure based on the likelihood; lower is better.


Factor
A categorical predictor in R that is represented by discrete levels.


False negative
A case where the true class is 1 but the model predicts 0.


False positive
A case where the true class is 0 but the model predicts 1.


Generalized linear model
A model class that combines a response distribution and a link function to relate predictors to the expected response.


Intercept
The expected log-odds when all predictors are set to zero (or to their reference values).


Interaction
A term that allows the effect of one predictor to depend on another.


Likelihood
A measure of how well a model explains the observed data; higher is better.


Likelihood ratio test
A test that compares nested models using differences in deviance.


Linear predictor
The linear combination of predictors, for example beta0 + beta1 x.


Link function
A function that connects the mean of the response to the linear predictor; for logistic regression the link is the logit.


Logit
The log of the odds: log(p / (1 - p)).


Logistic function
The S-shaped function that maps any real-valued input to a probability between 0 and 1.


Logistic regression
A regression model for binary outcomes that models the log-odds of an event as a linear function of predictors.


Maximum likelihood estimation
A method for estimating model parameters by choosing values that maximize the likelihood of the observed data.


Multivariable logistic regression
A logistic regression model with multiple predictors included simultaneously.


Nested models
Two models where one is a special case of the other (typically by removing one or more terms).


Null model
A model with no predictors, containing only an intercept.


Odds
The ratio of the probability of an event to the probability of it not occurring: p / (1 - p).


Odds ratio
The multiplicative change in odds for a one-unit increase in a predictor; exp(coefficient).


p-value
The probability, under a null hypothesis, of observing a result at least as extreme as the one obtained.


Predicted probability
The model’s estimate of the probability of an event for a given set of predictors.


Probability
A number between 0 and 1 that represents the chance of an event.


Pseudo R-squared
A likelihood-based summary of model fit for logistic regression that is used for rough comparison, not as a direct analogue of linear-model R-squared.


Random variable
A numerical variable whose value depends on the outcome of a random process.


Reference level
The baseline category for a factor against which other levels are compared.


ROC curve
A curve showing the trade-off between sensitivity and 1 - specificity across thresholds.


Saturated model
A model that fits the observed data perfectly by using as many parameters as needed.


Sensitivity
The proportion of true positives correctly identified by the model.


Separation
A situation where predictors perfectly separate outcomes, leading to unstable estimates.


Specificity
The proportion of true negatives correctly identified by the model.


True negative
A case where the true class is 0 and the model predicts 0.


True positive
A case where the true class is 1 and the model predicts 1.


Wald test
A hypothesis test that uses an estimated coefficient and its standard error to assess evidence against a null value.
